sections.title: 🧒 The Story
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
Manual console setup works once.
It fails when you need:
→ the same stack in dev, staging, and prod
→ disaster recovery in another region
→ peer review of infra changes
→ rollback after a bad change

CloudFormation solves that by making infrastructure declarative.

MENTAL MODEL:
You do not tell AWS "click this, then this."
You declare the desired end state:
→ VPC
→ subnets
→ ALB
→ ECS service
→ RDS

CloudFormation figures out creation order and keeps stack state.
```

---

blocks.content:
Manual console setup works once.
It fails when you need:
→ the same stack in dev, staging, and prod
→ disaster recovery in another region
→ peer review of infra changes
→ rollback after a bad change

CloudFormation solves that by making infrastructure declarative.

MENTAL MODEL:
You do not tell AWS "click this, then this."
You declare the desired end state:
→ VPC
→ subnets
→ ALB
→ ECS service
→ RDS

CloudFormation figures out creation order and keeps stack state.

revisionPoints: → the same stack in dev, staging, and prod
→ disaster recovery in another region
→ peer review of infra changes
→ rollback after a bad change
→ VPC
→ subnets
→ ALB
→ ECS service

sections.title: 🧱 Core Concepts
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
TEMPLATE:
→ YAML or JSON file describing resources

STACK:
→ a deployed instance of that template
→ same template can create dev/prod stacks with different parameters

CHANGE SET:
→ preview of what CloudFormation will change before executing

DRIFT:
→ real infrastructure no longer matches template
→ happens when someone changes resources manually in console

ROLLBACK:
→ if stack creation/update fails, CloudFormation rolls back changes automatically
```

### Example Template Shape
```
Parameters:
  Environment:
    Type: String
    AllowedValues: [dev, prod]

Resources:
  AppBucket:
    Type: AWS::S3::Bucket
    Properties:
      BucketName: !Sub myapp-${Environment}-assets

Outputs:
  BucketName:
    Value: !Ref AppBucket
```

---

blocks.content:
TEMPLATE:
→ YAML or JSON file describing resources

STACK:
→ a deployed instance of that template
→ same template can create dev/prod stacks with different parameters

CHANGE SET:
→ preview of what CloudFormation will change before executing

DRIFT:
→ real infrastructure no longer matches template
→ happens when someone changes resources manually in console

ROLLBACK:
→ if stack creation/update fails, CloudFormation rolls back changes automatically

Parameters:
  Environment:
    Type: String
    AllowedValues: [dev, prod]

Resources:
  AppBucket:
    Type: AWS::S3::Bucket
    Properties:
      BucketName: !Sub myapp-${Environment}-assets

Outputs:
  BucketName:
    Value: !Ref AppBucket

sections.title: 🧩 Advanced Patterns
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
NESTED STACKS:
→ split huge infrastructure into reusable parts
→ network stack, database stack, app stack

CROSS-STACK EXPORTS:
→ share outputs from one stack to another
→ VPC ID exported from network stack → imported by app stack

STACK POLICIES:
→ protect critical resources from accidental update/delete

DELETION POLICY:
→ Retain: keep resource after stack deletion
→ critical for databases, S3 buckets with important data

WAIT CONDITIONS / DEPENDS_ON:
→ control order when custom readiness matters
```

---

WAIT CONDITIONS / DEPENDS_ON:
→ control order when custom readiness matters

→ control order when custom readiness matters

sections.title: 💡 Best Practices
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
✅ one repo + review process for infra changes
✅ use parameters for environment-specific values, not copied templates
✅ use Change Sets before risky updates
✅ add DeletionPolicy: Retain for critical data stores
✅ run drift detection regularly
✅ never hotfix prod manually unless incident requires it — then reconcile template after
```

---

blocks.content:
✅ one repo + review process for infra changes
✅ use parameters for environment-specific values, not copied templates
✅ use Change Sets before risky updates
✅ add DeletionPolicy: Retain for critical data stores
✅ run drift detection regularly
✅ never hotfix prod manually unless incident requires it — then reconcile template after

sections.title: 🎯 Interview Q&A — CloudFormation
sections.rawMarkdown:
**Q: Why is Infrastructure as Code important beyond 'automation'?**
```
"Because the real value is control, repeatability, and safe change.

IaC gives:
→ peer review
→ version history
→ consistent environments
→ repeatable recovery
→ easier compliance evidence

Without IaC, every environment becomes a snowflake and outages become harder to reproduce or fix."
```

**Q: A CloudFormation stack update failed halfway. How do you handle that?**
```
"First I check stack events, not guesses.

I want to know:
→ which resource failed?
→ was rollback successful?
→ is stack in UPDATE_ROLLBACK_FAILED?

If rollback failed, I may need to continue rollback or repair the resource dependency first.
The senior move is not manual clicking everywhere; it is understanding stack state and getting
back to a clean declarative baseline."
```

**Q: What is drift and why is it dangerous?**
```
"Drift means the real resource differs from the template.

That is dangerous because:
→ future updates become unpredictable
→ disaster recovery recreates the wrong thing
→ compliance evidence becomes unreliable

Console changes feel fast, but they create hidden entropy.
Senior teams treat manual production edits as exceptions and reconcile them back into IaC quickly."
```

---

sections.title: 🔗 Connects To
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
CloudFormation → almost every AWS service: declarative provisioning
CloudFormation ← CI/CD: deploy templates as reviewed infrastructure changes
CloudFormation → IAM: execution roles and permissions for stack actions
CloudFormation → Outputs/Exports: connect stacks together

NEXT: CI/CD — CodePipeline, CodeBuild, CodeDeploy
How code and infrastructure changes move safely into production.
```

---

blocks.content:
CloudFormation → almost every AWS service: declarative provisioning
CloudFormation ← CI/CD: deploy templates as reviewed infrastructure changes
CloudFormation → IAM: execution roles and permissions for stack actions
CloudFormation → Outputs/Exports: connect stacks together

NEXT: CI/CD — CodePipeline, CodeBuild, CodeDeploy
How code and infrastructure changes move safely into production.

revisionPoints: CloudFormation → almost every AWS service: declarative provisioning
